stimulus:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

  • Sardine is a fish that contains cobalamin, an essential nutrient for humans.
  • Every 100 grams (g) of sardine contains 8.9 micrograms (mcg) of cobalamin.
  • Many bacteria can synthesize cobalamin, but humans cannot.
  • Humans must get cobalamin from foods, including fish and shellfish.
  • Cobalamin is also known as vitamin B12.
stem: The student wants to provide an example of a fish that contains vitamin B12. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

answerOptions.content: Vitamin B12, also known as cobalamin, can be found in sardine as well as other fish.
Humans cannot synthesize cobalamin in their bodies, but they can get it from foods, such as fish, for example.
Since humans cannot synthesize vitamin B12 in their bodies, they must get it from food.
Many bacteria can synthesize cobalamin, which is also known as vitamin B12, but humans cannot.

rationale:
Choice A is the best answer. The sentence provides an example of a fish that contains vitamin B12, noting that vitamin B12 (also known as cobalamin) can be found in sardine as well as other fish.

Choice B is incorrect. While the sentence explains that humans can get cobalamin from fish, it does not provide an example of a specific fish that contains vitamin B12. Choice C is incorrect. The sentence explains why humans must get vitamin B12 from foods; it does not provide an example of a fish that contains vitamin B12. Choice D is incorrect. The sentence compares cobalamin synthesis in bacteria with cobalamin synthesis in humans; it does not provide an example of a fish that contains vitamin B12.
